title: Shader Systems Practice Set
description: Backend-scored recall on vector math, lighting intuition, profiling, and practical optimization decisions.

questions.prompt: If two normalized vectors are perpendicular, what does their dot product evaluate to?
questions.explanation: For normalized vectors, the dot product becomes cos(theta). At 90 degrees, the result is 0.

questions.prompt: Which action is usually the best first step before optimizing a complex material?
questions.explanation: Profiling first prevents you from optimizing the wrong system or guessing at the dominant cost.

options.label: Reduce all texture sizes by half
Profile the scene and identify the dominant cost
Replace every function with custom HLSL

questions.prompt: A fresnel-style rim mask is commonly built from the relationship between which two vectors?
questions.explanation: A rim mask usually compares the surface normal against the view direction to measure how edge-on the surface is to camera.

questions.prompt: Which issue is often the dominant cost for layered translucent VFX in combat-heavy scenes?
questions.explanation: For translucent VFX, overdraw and fill rate are often more expensive than a few extra math instructions.

questions.prompt: Why do you usually normalize vectors before using them in a lighting dot product?
questions.explanation: Normalization removes magnitude, so the dot product becomes a clean read of directional alignment instead of mixing in vector length.

options.label: To make the shader compile faster
To compare direction instead of direction plus length
To convert tangent space into UV space

questions.prompt: What is the strongest production reason to add a cheaper fallback version of a shader?
questions.explanation: A fallback helps preserve the creative goal while giving you a scalable version for weaker hardware or non-hero usage.

options.label: It removes the need to profile
It gives you a scalable option without deleting the effect entirely
It guarantees identical visual quality on every platform

questions.prompt: In shader language, what is the most practical way to describe the dot product?
questions.explanation: The dot product is often best explained as a cheap alignment test between two directions.

options.label: A cheap measure of how aligned two directions are
A conversion from world space to tangent space
A way to increase vertex density
